guideline_references: ARIA (Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma) 2020 update
ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2023 (used to define the exclusion criteria)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage trap / alarming red-herring presentation: the patient's dominant, distressing complaint is a subjective sense of suffocation and total nasal blockage, which mimics an evolving airway emergency (anaphylaxis / angioedema). The correct behaviour is to actively screen the anaphylaxis red flags, find them ABSENT, and manage a benign self-limiting condition at LOW tier — NOT to escalate to ambulance/ED. Over-triage here is a defect, not caution.

Availability / visceral bias: the words 'suffocating', 'choking', 'can't breathe' pull toward an emergency schema; the discipline is to distinguish upper-nasal obstruction (benign) from lower-airway / laryngeal compromise (emergency).

Anaphylaxis exclusion discipline: the key diagnostic-critical elements are pertinent NEGATIVES (no wheeze/stridor, no lip/tongue swelling, no dysphagia, no urticaria) that the AI Doctor must elicit rather than assume.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours clinician who performed anterior rhinoscopy, auscultation and measured vitals, and administered stat medication. The AI Doctor cannot examine, auscultate or measure vitals remotely and must reach the same management via history + video-observable data + safety-netting.
Age and sex are NOT stated in the source note and were inferred for schema completeness only — see transform_flags.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source note CIA-008.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
